medications.indication_rationale: Low-dose aspirin is standard antiplatelet secondary prevention in symptomatic peripheral arterial disease to reduce cardiovascular events. In the source note the in-person clinician initiated it; in telehealth this is a reasonable recommendation, but initiation should be confirmed at the urgent in-person review that this case requires.
contraindications_in_this_case: active peptic ulcer or GI bleeding

medications.indication_rationale: High-intensity statin therapy is the definitive treatment for the severe underlying lipid disorder and to stabilise atherosclerotic plaque in established peripheral arterial disease. The source note defers commencement to the GP review after fasting lipids; no specific dose is given in the note, so none is stated here (dose to be set at in-person review against the fasting lipid result).

May improve pain-free walking distance in intermittent claudication when a structured exercise program and risk-factor control are insufficient; a specialist/vascular decision, not first-line and not started via telehealth. No dose stated (not in the source note).
contraindications_in_this_case: heart failure (contraindicated) — must be excluded before use

medications.indication_rationale: The presentation is arterial claudication, not venous thromboembolism; anticoagulating for a presumed DVT is an error of commission that both misses the true diagnosis and exposes the patient to bleeding risk without benefit.

medications.drug_name: opioid analgesia for the leg cramps
medications.indication_rationale: Claudication pain is treated by revascularisation, exercise and risk-factor control, not analgesia; opioids do not address the ischaemia and risk dependence and adverse effects.

allied_health_referrals.rationale: A structured/supervised exercise program is a cornerstone of claudication management, improving pain-free walking distance by promoting collateral vessel growth.
Dietary change to reduce saturated fat and processed food intake supports lipid lowering and cardiovascular risk reduction.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Begin a structured walking program: walk to the point of moderate calf pain, stop and rest until it fully resolves, then continue; build the pain-free distance progressively.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Intentionally exercising to the pain threshold and resting stimulates growth of new collateral blood vessels, measurably increasing walking distance over time.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Shift from processed/packaged meals toward a heart-healthy diet lower in saturated fat.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Supports lipid lowering and reduces atherosclerotic progression.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: If the patient smokes, offer smoking-cessation support as a priority.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Smoking is the strongest modifiable driver of peripheral arterial disease progression and limb loss.
behavioural_change_actions.feasibility_note: Smoking status was not documented in the source note — establish it at review.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Support gradual weight reduction alongside increased activity.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Reduces cardiovascular risk and improves exercise tolerance.

patient_education_points.point: The leg cramps are a warning sign that the arteries supplying the legs are narrowed by plaque; when the muscles need more blood during walking, the narrowed vessels cannot deliver it, causing the pain.

This same plaque affects arteries throughout the body, so the risk of heart attack and stroke is high without medication and lifestyle change — this is why treatment matters even though the legs are the only symptom now.
Pace your walking: walk until the pain starts, then rest until it eases, then continue — over time you should be able to walk further before it starts.
Diagnosing the exact lipid disorder needs a fasting blood test — fast overnight and have the blood drawn first thing in the morning.
Go to hospital immediately if the foot becomes cold, pale or numb, or if the leg hurts continuously while resting — these can mean the blood supply has been suddenly cut off.

follow_up_plan.interval: Urgent same-day in-person assessment for pulse examination and to exclude acute limb ischaemia; then GP review within ~3 days for fasting lipid results and to commence high-intensity statin, with an urgent vascular referral (ABI and vascular surgery) in parallel.
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Distal pulses and limb viability, ABI, fasting lipids/HbA1c, blood pressure, cardiovascular risk, statin commencement and walking-program progress.
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: Any rest pain, night pain, or the foot becoming cold, pale, numb or weak — seek emergency care immediately rather than waiting for the scheduled review.

must_include_items: Recognise the reproducible exertional, rest-relieved calf pain as a vascular (arterial claudication) pattern rather than a benign chronic lipid problem
Screen for acute limb ischaemia (rest pain, pallor, coldness, numbness, weakness)
Arrange an urgent same-day in-person assessment because pulses and ABI cannot be checked via telehealth
Arrange urgent vascular referral (ABI and vascular surgery) and fasting lipid profile
Recommend high-intensity statin and low-dose aspirin (initiation confirmed in person) plus a structured walking program
Safety-net explicitly for the 6 Ps of acute limb ischaemia with immediate ED/000 escalation
acceptable_alternatives: Referral to urgent care rather than the GP for the same-day pulse examination is acceptable if faster
Deferring statin dose selection to the in-person review after fasting lipids is acceptable and expected
errors_of_omission: Failing to arrange any urgent in-person assessment and managing purely as a remote lipid problem
Failing to screen for or safety-net acute limb ischaemia
Failing to recommend the structured walking program
errors_of_commission: Anticoagulating or ordering a DVT work-up for presumed venous thrombosis
Prescribing opioids for the leg cramps
Inventing or stating a specific statin dose not supported by the source or a fasting lipid result
Reassuring the patient that a warm, pink foot at rest excludes serious arterial disease
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: A pass REQUIRES recognising the vascular (arterial) pattern AND arranging urgent in-person assessment with explicit safety-netting for acute limb ischaemia. Managing this as a routine remote lipid consult, or excluding acute limb ischaemia on the basis of a self-reported warm foot, is a critical failure.
